hook.text: A pizza shop charges $12 per pizza. Does anyone know what each pizza actually costs to make?

buildup.text: Cost accounting tracks the money spent on producing goods or delivering services.

discovery.text: It breaks costs into ingredients, labor, rent, utilities, and overhead—revealing the true cost per unit.

twist.text: Many businesses set prices by gut feeling. Cost accounting replaces guessing with data.

climax.text: Without it, a company might sell products for less than they cost—and slowly go bankrupt.

punchline.text: You can't price right if you don't know your costs.

quiz.question: What does cost accounting track?
options: The cost of producing goods or services

hook.text: The flour in a loaf of bread is a direct cost. The bakery's electricity bill is indirect.

buildup.text: Direct costs can be traced to a specific product—materials, labor on that item.

discovery.text: Indirect costs (overhead) benefit many products—rent, insurance, management salaries.

twist.text: Allocating overhead to products is tricky. Different methods give different costs per unit.

climax.text: Getting the split right determines which products appear profitable and which don't.

punchline.text: Direct costs are obvious. Indirect costs hide everywhere.

quiz.question: What is an indirect cost?
options: A cost that benefits many products and can't be traced to one
The raw material in a product
A cost paid directly to suppliers

hook.text: A gym pays $10,000 rent whether one person works out or a thousand. That's a fixed cost.

buildup.text: Fixed costs don't change with production volume—rent, salaries, insurance stay constant.

discovery.text: Variable costs rise and fall with output—raw materials, packaging, sales commissions.

twist.text: A business with high fixed costs needs lots of sales to break even—but profits soar after that point.

climax.text: Understanding cost structure helps managers predict how profits change as sales fluctuate.

punchline.text: Fixed costs wait. Variable costs follow every sale.

quiz.question: What is a fixed cost?
options: A cost that stays the same regardless of production volume
A cost that changes with each unit produced

hook.text: Revenue looks great until you subtract what it cost to deliver. That's cost of goods sold.

buildup.text: COGS includes raw materials, direct labor, and manufacturing overhead tied to products sold.

discovery.text: Gross profit = Revenue minus COGS. It shows how much money production itself generates.

twist.text: A company with $10 million in revenue but $9.5 million in COGS barely has room to breathe.

climax.text: Managing COGS is where operational efficiency meets financial performance.

punchline.text: Revenue is vanity. COGS reveals the real margin.

quiz.question: What does COGS measure?
options: The direct cost of producing goods that were sold

hook.text: A factory makes chairs and tables. The rent covers both. How do you split it fairly?

buildup.text: Overhead allocation assigns indirect costs to products using a systematic method.

discovery.text: Common methods use machine hours, labor hours, or square footage as the allocation base.

twist.text: The method you choose changes which products look profitable—and that changes business decisions.

climax.text: Activity-based costing offers a more precise approach by tracing overhead to specific activities.

punchline.text: How you split the bill changes who looks expensive.

quiz.question: What is overhead allocation?
options: Assigning indirect costs to products systematically

hook.text: Produce 100 widgets and each costs $10. Produce 10,000 and each costs $3. What happened?

buildup.text: As volume rises, fixed costs spread across more units—lowering the cost per unit.

discovery.text: This is economies of scale—the more you produce, the cheaper each item becomes.

twist.text: But push too far and diseconomies appear—overtime, crowding, and quality problems raise costs again.

climax.text: Finding the sweet spot of production volume is a core challenge for every manufacturer.

punchline.text: More volume, lower cost—until you overdo it.

quiz.question: Why does cost per unit fall as volume increases?
options: Fixed costs spread across more units

hook.text: A startup sells candles for $20 each. It costs $8 per candle plus $6,000 monthly rent. When does it profit?

buildup.text: The break-even point is where total revenue exactly equals total costs—zero profit, zero loss.

discovery.text: Break-even units = Fixed costs ÷ (Price – Variable cost). Here: $6,000 ÷ $12 = 500 candles.

twist.text: Selling 499 candles means a loss. Selling 501 means profit. One sale can flip the outcome.

climax.text: Every business should know its break-even—it's the survival threshold.

punchline.text: Below break-even, you're paying to work.

quiz.question: What is the break-even point?
options: Where total revenue equals total costs

hook.text: Each latte sells for $5 and costs $1.50 to make. That $3.50 leftover is the contribution margin.

buildup.text: Contribution margin = Price minus variable cost. It's what each sale contributes to covering fixed costs.

discovery.text: Once fixed costs are covered, every additional contribution margin dollar goes straight to profit.

twist.text: A product with thin margins needs massive volume. A product with fat margins can profit with fewer sales.

climax.text: Managers use contribution margin to decide pricing, product mix, and whether to drop weak products.

punchline.text: Margin per unit tells you if each sale is worth making.

quiz.question: What does contribution margin measure?
options: How much each sale contributes to covering fixed costs

hook.text: A call center handles 500 calls per agent. At 501 calls, you need another agent—costs jump.

buildup.text: Step costs stay flat within a range but jump to a new level when capacity is exceeded.

discovery.text: Hiring staff, renting warehouse space, and buying machines all create step-cost patterns.

twist.text: Right after a step increase, each unit costs much more—until volume fills the new capacity.

climax.text: Smart managers time growth to stay near the top of each step—maximizing use of paid capacity.

punchline.text: Costs don't always climb gradually. Sometimes they jump.

quiz.question: What are step costs?
options: Costs that stay flat then jump at capacity limits
Costs that increase with every unit

hook.text: Your phone plan costs $30/month plus $0.10 per extra minute. Fixed and variable combined.

buildup.text: Mixed costs have both a fixed component and a variable component that changes with activity.

discovery.text: Utilities are classic mixed costs—a base charge plus usage fees that rise with consumption.

twist.text: Separating the fixed and variable parts of mixed costs is essential for accurate analysis.

climax.text: The high-low method and regression analysis help accountants split mixed costs into their parts.

punchline.text: Some costs are fixed. Some are variable. Some are both.

quiz.question: What is a mixed cost?
options: A cost with both fixed and variable components
A cost that only occurs occasionally
A cost shared between two departments

hook.text: A company without a budget is like a pilot without a flight plan—flying blind.

buildup.text: A budget is a financial plan that sets spending limits and revenue targets for a future period.

discovery.text: Budgets force managers to think ahead, prioritize spending, and coordinate across departments.

twist.text: The process of creating the budget is often more valuable than the final numbers themselves.

climax.text: Companies that budget consistently outperform those that don't—because planning drives discipline.

punchline.text: A budget doesn't limit you. It shows you what's possible.

quiz.question: What is the primary purpose of a budget?
options: To plan spending and set financial targets

hook.text: A company creates dozens of smaller budgets. They all roll up into one master plan.

buildup.text: The master budget combines sales, production, purchasing, labor, overhead, and capital budgets.

discovery.text: It produces a projected income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement for the period.

twist.text: If the sales budget is wrong, every other budget built on it will be wrong too.

climax.text: The master budget is the company's financial blueprint—every department contributes a piece.

punchline.text: One plan unites every department's numbers.

quiz.question: What does a master budget combine?
options: All individual department budgets into one plan

hook.text: You budgeted for 10,000 units but sold 12,000. Is your spending over budget or in line?

buildup.text: A static budget is set once and doesn't change. A flexible budget adjusts for actual volume.

discovery.text: Flexible budgets recalculate expected costs based on real output—giving a fairer comparison.

twist.text: Without flexible budgets, a manager who sells more looks like a bigger spender—even if costs are in line.

climax.text: Flexible budgets separate volume effects from efficiency, showing where real problems lie.

punchline.text: Fair comparison requires a budget that flexes with reality.

quiz.question: What does a flexible budget adjust for?

hook.text: Most budgets start with last year's numbers. Zero-based budgeting starts from scratch—every time.

buildup.text: Every expense must be justified from zero. No department gets funding just because it did last year.

discovery.text: This forces managers to rethink priorities and eliminate spending that no longer adds value.

twist.text: It's thorough but exhausting—many organizations try it once and go back to traditional methods.

climax.text: When done well, zero-based budgeting can find millions in savings hidden by routine spending.

punchline.text: Start from zero. Justify every dollar. Keep only what matters.

quiz.question: How does zero-based budgeting differ from traditional budgeting?
options: Every expense must be justified from zero each period

hook.text: You budgeted $100,000 for materials but spent $115,000. Why? Variance analysis finds out.

buildup.text: Variance is the difference between budgeted and actual results. It can be favorable or unfavorable.

discovery.text: Breaking it down reveals whether the issue was price (paid too much) or quantity (used too much).

twist.text: A favorable variance isn't always good—buying cheap materials might lead to costly defects later.

climax.text: Managers investigate significant variances and take corrective action before small gaps become big ones.

punchline.text: Variances ask: what went wrong, and why?

quiz.question: What does variance analysis compare?
options: This year's revenue to last year's

hook.text: You already spent $50,000 developing a product. Should that affect your decision to launch it?

buildup.text: Sunk costs are money already spent that can't be recovered. They shouldn't influence future decisions.

discovery.text: Relevant costs are future costs that differ between alternatives—only these should guide choices.

twist.text: Human nature clings to sunk costs. The 'we've invested too much to quit' trap destroys value.

climax.text: Smart decision-making ignores the past and focuses on what changes going forward.

punchline.text: Don't let yesterday's spending ruin tomorrow's decision.

quiz.question: What are sunk costs?
options: Past costs that can't be recovered
Future costs that vary by decision

hook.text: Should a car company make its own seats or buy them from a supplier? The numbers decide.

buildup.text: Make-or-buy analysis compares the full cost of in-house production against purchasing externally.

discovery.text: Include materials, labor, overhead, quality control—and the opportunity cost of using that capacity.

twist.text: Buying might be cheaper, but outsourcing risks quality issues and supply chain dependency.

climax.text: The best decisions weigh both the numbers and the strategic implications of each option.

punchline.text: Cheaper isn't always better. Cost is only part of the answer.

quiz.question: What does make-or-buy analysis compare?
options: In-house production cost vs. external purchase cost

hook.text: A hotel chain offers to buy 5,000 pillows at $8 each. Your normal price is $15. Accept or refuse?

buildup.text: Special order analysis asks: will this order contribute to profit, even at a lower price?

discovery.text: If the price exceeds variable cost and there's spare capacity, the order adds to profit.

twist.text: But if regular customers learn about the discount, they'll demand the same price—eroding margins.

climax.text: The decision depends on capacity, variable costs, and whether the deal stays confidential.

punchline.text: Below list price can still mean above zero profit.

quiz.question: When should a company accept a special order below normal price?
options: When the price exceeds variable cost and spare capacity exists

hook.text: You use your warehouse for storage. You could rent it out for $5,000 a month. That's your opportunity cost.

buildup.text: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative you give up when making a choice.

discovery.text: It doesn't appear in accounting records, but it's real—and ignoring it leads to bad decisions.

twist.text: A college degree has an opportunity cost: the four years of salary you gave up while studying.

climax.text: Every resource has an alternative use. Effective managers factor in what they're sacrificing.

punchline.text: What you chose to skip is part of what your choice cost.

quiz.question: What is opportunity cost?
options: The value of the next best alternative given up
The price of the cheapest option
A cost that occurs in the future

topics.title: Keep or Drop a Product Line

hook.text: The bakery's muffin line loses $2,000 a month. Should you drop it? Not so fast.

buildup.text: Dropping a product eliminates its revenue and variable costs—but fixed costs often remain.

discovery.text: If the muffin line covers its variable costs and part of the fixed costs, dropping it could make things worse.

twist.text: Customers who come for muffins also buy coffee and pastries. Losing them could shrink total sales.

climax.text: The decision requires analyzing contribution margin, avoidable costs, and customer behavior.

punchline.text: A losing product might still be carrying its weight.

quiz.question: Why might a company keep a money-losing product line?
options: It may still cover variable costs and some fixed costs

hook.text: A dashboard full of numbers means nothing if you don't know which ones matter.

buildup.text: KPIs are specific metrics that track progress toward a company's most important goals.

discovery.text: Financial KPIs include return on investment, profit margin, and revenue growth rate.

twist.text: Too many KPIs dilute focus. The best companies track five to ten that truly drive performance.

climax.text: Good KPIs are measurable, actionable, and directly connected to strategic objectives.

punchline.text: Measure what matters. Ignore the noise.

quiz.question: What makes a good KPI?
options: It's measurable, actionable, and tied to strategy
It only looks at past performance

hook.text: You invest $100,000 in a project that earns $20,000 profit. Is that good? ROI says it's 20%.

buildup.text: ROI = Profit ÷ Investment. It measures how efficiently capital is being used to generate returns.

discovery.text: A higher ROI means each invested dollar works harder. Managers compare ROI across projects.

twist.text: Chasing ROI can make managers reject good projects that would grow the company but lower the ratio.

climax.text: ROI is powerful but imperfect—it should be combined with other metrics for balanced decisions.

punchline.text: ROI tells you if your money is working hard enough.

quiz.question: What does ROI measure?
options: How fast a company is growing

hook.text: Profit alone doesn't tell the full story. A balanced scorecard looks at four dimensions.

buildup.text: It measures financial results, customer satisfaction, internal processes, and learning and growth.

discovery.text: A company might be profitable today but failing at innovation—threatening its future.

twist.text: Kaplan and Norton created the balanced scorecard in 1992. It's still one of the most used strategy tools.

climax.text: The framework forces managers to think beyond short-term profits and invest in long-term health.

punchline.text: Four perspectives. One complete picture of performance.

quiz.question: How many perspectives does the balanced scorecard use?

hook.text: A division manager controls sales and costs but not corporate taxes. Judge them on what they control.

buildup.text: Responsibility accounting evaluates managers based only on the items they can influence.

discovery.text: Cost centers control costs. Profit centers control costs and revenue. Investment centers control all three.

twist.text: Blaming a manager for costs outside their control destroys morale and hides the real problem.

climax.text: Fair evaluation requires matching authority with accountability—no more, no less.

punchline.text: Judge managers on what they can control. Nothing more.

quiz.question: What does responsibility accounting evaluate?
options: Managers based on items they can control
All managers on total company profit

hook.text: Division A makes parts for Division B. What price should A charge? Too high or low distorts both.

buildup.text: Transfer pricing sets the cost of goods or services exchanged between divisions of the same company.

discovery.text: Options include market price, cost-plus, or negotiated price—each has trade-offs.

twist.text: Wrong transfer prices make profitable divisions look bad and struggling ones look good—corrupting decisions.

climax.text: The goal is a price that motivates both divisions to act in the company's best interest.

punchline.text: Internal prices shape internal behavior. Get them right.

quiz.question: What is internal transfer pricing?
options: The price charged between divisions of the same company
The price charged to external customers

hook.text: Two products use the same machine hours but different amounts of setup time. Traditional costing treats them the same.

buildup.text: Activity-based costing assigns overhead based on the actual activities that drive costs.

discovery.text: It identifies cost drivers—setups, inspections, orders—and traces costs to products that consume them.

twist.text: ABC often reveals that high-volume products subsidize low-volume ones—a hidden cross-subsidy.

climax.text: More accurate product costs lead to better pricing, better mix decisions, and higher profits.

punchline.text: Costs should follow the activities that cause them.

quiz.question: What does activity-based costing use to assign overhead?
options: The actual activities that drive costs

hook.text: Toyota doesn't set a price after calculating cost. It sets a target cost from the market price.

buildup.text: Target costing starts with what customers will pay and subtracts the desired profit margin.

discovery.text: The result is a maximum allowable cost. Engineers must design the product to hit that number.

twist.text: This reverses traditional thinking—the market sets the cost limit, not the accounting department.

climax.text: Japanese manufacturers pioneered this approach and it's now used across industries worldwide.

punchline.text: Start with the price. Work backward to the cost.

quiz.question: How is a target cost determined?

hook.text: Traditional accounting tracks every labor minute. Lean accounting says: stop counting, start flowing.

buildup.text: Lean accounting supports lean manufacturing—eliminating waste and focusing on value streams.

discovery.text: Instead of tracking individual product costs, it measures the performance of entire value streams.

twist.text: Standard costing can actually encourage overproduction—making more to absorb overhead looks good on paper.

climax.text: Lean accounting aligns financial reporting with operational reality—what actually creates value.

punchline.text: Stop producing waste just to make the numbers look good.

quiz.question: What does lean accounting focus on?

hook.text: A cheap printer costs $50. But ink cartridges over its life cost $500. Which is the real cost?

buildup.text: Life-cycle costing captures all costs from design through disposal—not just the purchase price.

discovery.text: It includes R&D, production, marketing, warranty, maintenance, and end-of-life disposal.

twist.text: Products that look cheap at launch can be wildly expensive over their full life cycle.

climax.text: Smart buyers and smart managers evaluate total cost of ownership, not sticker price.

punchline.text: The purchase price is just the first installment.

quiz.question: What does life-cycle costing include?
options: All costs from design through disposal

hook.text: Cut one cent per unit per month. After a year, that's twelve cents—multiplied by millions of units.

buildup.text: Kaizen costing pursues continuous, incremental cost reductions during the production phase.

discovery.text: Workers on the floor suggest small improvements—a better layout, fewer steps, less waste.

twist.text: No single change is dramatic, but compounded over years, the savings are enormous.

climax.text: Toyota and other Japanese firms built global dominance partly through relentless kaizen discipline.

punchline.text: Small daily improvements add up to massive yearly savings.

quiz.question: What is the core idea of kaizen costing?
options: Continuous small cost reductions over time
